prompt: Explain the water cycle, in prose, without using any lists, bullets, headings, or numbered steps. Keep it to one paragraph.
response: Water is constantly moving through a continuous cycle that connects the ocean, atmosphere, and land. It begins as liquid water in oceans, lakes, and rivers, where heat from the sun causes the surface water to evaporate, transforming it into water vapor that rises into the atmosphere. As this vapor climbs to higher, cooler altitudes, it condenses around tiny particles of dust and other matter to form clouds and fog. When enough water droplets combine and grow heavy enough, they fall back to Earth as precipitation in the form of rain, snow, sleet, or hail. Once on the ground, some of this water flows across the surface as runoff, making its way into streams and rivers that eventually lead back to the ocean, while some soaks into the soil and becomes groundwater, slowly moving through underground aquifers. Plants also play a role by absorbing groundwater through their roots and releasing it back into the air through their leaves in a process called transpiration. All of these processes work together in an endless loop, redistributing fresh water across the planet and making life on Earth possible.
